global_notes: Keep the images calm, premium, and clear on a documentation-heavy website. Favor strong silhouettes, clean geometry, and a travel-compliance tone over busy scenes or stock-photo energy.

slots.scene_brief: Show the AtlasDays map as the main subject: visited countries glowing, one selected country or region visibly focused, and a small supporting stat or trip-summary card tying the map back to the travel history. It should feel exploratory and tactile, not like a generic analytics dashboard.
must_include: one clearly emphasized country or region
a small secondary summary element connected to the map
must_avoid: generic dashboard-overview composition repeated from the hero

slots.anchor_heading: Choose the right trip mode first
slots.scene_brief: Show the first important product decision in AtlasDays: choosing between Exact Dates and approximate trip history. Use two distinct mode cards or panels that lead into different timeline outcomes, making the tradeoff visually clear without requiring body text.
must_include: a downstream timeline or history effect showing why the choice matters
must_avoid: large blocks of explanatory copy inside the image

help/history-and-import.notes: Make the contrast between exact dates and year-only or unknown trips visually obvious without relying on readable UI text.

slots.scene_brief: Show AtlasDays turning messy travel memory into a clear system: one side has softer year-only or uncertain trip blocks, the other has crisp exact-date trip cards, and both feed into one elegant timeline. It should feel like a premium travel app solving a real problem, not a filing cabinet.
must_include: both approximate and exact trip shapes
a single timeline or record system connecting them
subtle travel movement or route context

slots.scene_brief: Show an exact-date trip as the primary subject with crisp start and end anchors, clear duration, and actionable structure. Keep a softer year-only trip as a secondary comparison so it is obvious why exact dates are more powerful for counting and planning.
must_include: one exact trip card or band as the main focus
one softer approximate trip representation as a comparison
slots.notes: Show why exact dates are more actionable while still hinting that approximate trips remain useful.

help/privacy-location-and-sync.notes: Focus on local-device privacy and optional sync. Do not use surveillance or creepy location-tracking imagery.

slots.scene_brief: Show a private device detecting a country change locally as travel crosses a border or route boundary. The device should be the anchor, with subtle country-shift or border-crossing cues and a calm privacy halo that implies the work happens on-device.
must_avoid: cloud-only sync visuals dominating the image

help/trackers-and-limits.notes: The image should make rolling windows, yearly thresholds, and per-stay counting feel structured and understandable.

slots.scene_brief: Show one travel history powering several different tracker shapes at once: a rolling window, a yearly target, and a per-stay counter, all connected to the same trip timeline. It should feel like a premium travel intelligence system, not a tax chart.
must_include: one shared travel timeline or trip history source
clear visual hierarchy with one primary tracker and two supporting trackers

slots.scene_brief: Show a structured set of distinct tracker presets as elegant cards or tiles: Residence, Schengen, Visa Limit, Travel Goal, and Custom. Each one should feel visibly different through its internal geometry, thresholds, or window shape so the idea of choosing the right preset is immediately clear without reading labels.
must_include: five distinct option cards or tiles
one option visually highlighted as selected
different counting shapes or window metaphors across the options
must_avoid: generic world map dominating the image

slots.scene_brief: Show a year-based presence threshold filling toward a decisive residency marker. Use calendar blocks, a year ring, or structured day accumulation so it is obvious the rule is about cumulative presence over a defined year, not one isolated trip.
must_avoid: tourist-route map as the primary subject
tax form or legal paperwork visuals

slots.scene_brief: Show several jurisdictions or country nodes linked by the same 183-day concept, making it clear that multiple countries use this kind of threshold but not always in identical ways.
must_avoid: dense legal text or treaty-style imagery

slots.scene_brief: Show the same world being interpreted through multiple counting systems at once, with different list lenses or segmented overlays producing different totals. The main point is disagreement between systems, not one definitive number.
must_include: one shared globe or world map
multiple competing frames or list systems
clear visual disagreement between the counts
must_avoid: one flat globe without comparison structure

slots.scene_brief: Show several country-list systems as a structured set of choice cards, each tied to a different use case such as everyday reference, sports, travelers, or regional exploration. It should feel like a practical selection guide rather than a geography poster.
must_include: subtle use-case cues attached to each option

slots.anchor_heading: Popular destinations and their day limits
slots.scene_brief: Show several popular nomad destinations as premium travel cards with visibly different day-limit or stay-shape rules. It should feel aspirational enough for travel content while still centered on the practical limit differences.
must_include: different limit shapes or counters per destination
subtle travel atmosphere rather than dry admin art
must_avoid: one generic world map with no destination identity

slots.scene_brief: Show how multiple legal or practical counters overlap for a nomad: visa limits, tax-presence thresholds, and rolling windows all intersecting over one travel path. The point should be complexity and overlap, not a single clean rule.
must_include: one shared travel path or timeline

slots.scene_brief: Show one layover situation split into two interpretations: the legal or immigration question on one side, and the personal travel-list question on the other. Make the fork in logic the main message.
must_include: airport or transit cues without becoming stock-photo travel art

slots.scene_brief: Show the difference between staying inside transit space and actually stepping into a place. One side should feel like sealed airport transit, the other like a minimal but real encounter with the city or country beyond the airport.
must_include: a sense of threshold or crossing between the two

slots.anchor_heading: The usual pain of compiling travel history
slots.scene_brief: Show the frustrating reality of assembling travel history from scattered confirmations, stamps, notes, and bookings. It should feel messy but still readable, with a sense that the records could be pulled together into order.
must_include: a hint of structure emerging from the mess
must_avoid: neat finished export table as the main subject

slots.anchor_heading: What to include in your travel history
slots.scene_brief: Show a clean, reusable travel-history export structure with fields like country, entry, exit, purpose, and notes represented abstractly as tidy rows or cards. It should feel practical and submission-ready without imitating an official form.

Show a clean sovereign-state world view anchored around the familiar 195-country answer. The composition should feel definitive but simple, more like a crisp reference point than a full comparison chart.
must_avoid: multiple competing counts in the same image

slots.scene_brief: Show why country totals vary by breaking the world into different list logics such as sovereign states, territories, and travel-focused regions. The image should make variation feel inevitable, not confusing.
must_include: visible difference between the resulting totals
must_avoid: flat textbook globe with no comparison structure

slots.scene_brief: Show a spreadsheet-like manual tracking system for travel days, with rows, columns, and trip spans laid out cleanly enough to look useful but obviously manual. It should feel workable, not mocked.
must_avoid: messy failed-spreadsheet chaos as the only message

slots.scene_brief: Show the weak points of spreadsheet tracking: overlapping trips, changing windows, and edge-case counting that become hard to manage by hand. The image should communicate friction and fragility without becoming visually messy.
must_include: a sense of rolling calculations changing over time

slots.scene_brief: Show the act of logging a trip in AtlasDays as a clean travel record flowing into the timeline. It should feel lightweight and product-led, with just enough structure to imply how trips are captured without recreating the literal UI.
must_include: travel context such as route dots or country markers
must_avoid: full product overview repeated from hero

slots.scene_brief: Show the Schengen tracker as a focused rule card with a rolling window, remaining-days logic, and a European travel context. It should feel actionable rather than abstract.
must_include: one strong tracker card as the main subject

slots.scene_brief: Show AtlasDays syncing private travel history between devices through a clean, controlled iCloud-like bridge. The relationship between device, cloud, and the same travel record should be obvious, but the mood should stay minimal and privacy-first.
must_include: one shared travel record or timeline

slots.scene_brief: Show a travel record continuing seamlessly across devices in real-world use: one trip history, multiple screens, no messy duplication. It should feel convenient and modern, not like enterprise device management.
must_include: multiple devices in one connected system
one consistent travel timeline or tracker state

slots.scene_brief: Show one trip supported by two tiers of proof: stronger primary evidence and weaker supporting evidence. The composition should make the hierarchy intuitive without feeling accusatory or forensic.
must_include: one shared trip or time-frame tying them together

slots.scene_brief: Show a clean, reusable evidence template for proving time spent in a country, with fields for country, dates, source, and confidence represented as structured rows or cards. It should feel practical and organized, not like an official form.
must_include: a clear confidence or certainty cue
must_avoid: messy evidence scraps as the main subject

learn/rebuild-travel-history-from-passpo.notes: Lean into reconstruction from scattered evidence: photos, tickets, emails, stamps, and notes becoming one coherent timeline.

slots.scene_brief: Show the starter kit for rebuilding travel history: passport stamps, emails, tickets, photo markers, and notes arranged as the first organized evidence set. It should feel like the beginning of a reconstruction, not a completed report.
must_avoid: finished timeline as the only subject

slots.scene_brief: Show scattered travel evidence progressively turning into an ordered timeline. The sequence should make the reconstruction process feel achievable and methodical.
must_include: partial dates or uncertain entries resolving into cleaner records
must_avoid: one frozen end-state with no sense of process

learn/schengen-90-180-rule.notes: Europe should be instantly recognizable. Emphasize the rolling-window idea more than passports or airport interiors.

slots.scene_brief: Show a horizontal timeline or calendar strip with a visibly sliding 180-day frame. The main story should be days falling out on one side while the active count stays inside the moving window.
must_include: counted days inside the active range
days visibly falling out behind the current date

slots.anchor_heading: Common scenarios that trip people up
slots.scene_brief: Show two or three common Schengen mistake patterns as separate panels, such as repeated short trips, boundary-straddling stays, or nearby countries that count differently. The image should teach through contrast.
must_avoid: one generalized map without scenario separation

slots.scene_brief: Show the current Schengen Area as a distinct highlighted European cluster, with neighboring non-Schengen countries muted or treated differently. The point is membership clarity, not general Europe travel vibes.
Show why the Schengen membership list matters in practice by contrasting routes or nearby destinations that look close together geographically but do not count the same way under the 90/180 rule.
must_include: one route that counts and one that does not
must_avoid: generic membership map repeated from body-1
dense legal explanation inside the image

slots.scene_brief: Show a day-by-day rolling-window example where the active count changes as the timeline advances. This should feel like a concrete walkthrough rather than a generic abstract rule image.
must_avoid: broad hero overview repeated from the top image

slots.scene_brief: Show common rolling-window misunderstandings as separate panels, such as assuming a fixed reset date or miscounting overlapping trips. The image should make the mistakes visually obvious.
must_include: visual contrast between wrong and right states

slots.scene_brief: Show the typical pieces of travel history a visa application cares about: country, entry, exit, purpose, and supporting notes or sources. The image should feel like a clear checklist of expected fields without imitating a government form.
Show the workflow of compiling travel history from multiple sources into one clean application-ready record. It should feel methodical and reassuring rather than burdensome.
must_include: source materials turning into a clean record
must_avoid: one static table with no process

learn/travel-history-template-visa-appli.notes: Favor tidy organization, timeline structure, and travel records. Avoid anything that looks like an official government form.

slots.scene_brief: Show a premium travel-history composition where trip records, route traces, date cards, and a subtle world-travel context come together into one clean organized system. It should feel useful and aspirational, not bureaucratic.

Show a clean reusable travel-history template as an elegant table or card system with consistent fields for country, arrival, departure, and notes. The image should feel like an organized framework someone could actually model their records on, without rendering literal readable table text.
must_include: date and country fields represented abstractly

slots.anchor_heading: What to include if your records are incomplete
slots.scene_brief: Show incomplete travel evidence being turned into a usable record: partial tickets, note cards, passport fragments, photo markers, and uncertain date blocks gradually resolving into one coherent timeline. It should feel practical and hopeful rather than chaotic.
must_include: a timeline or structured record emerging from them

slots.scene_brief: Show two entry routes into the UK side by side: the lighter ETA path and the fuller Standard Visitor visa path. The image should communicate route choice and relative complexity without relying on long text labels.

slots.anchor_heading: When you may need a visa instead
slots.scene_brief: Show the kinds of situations that push a traveler from ETA territory into needing a visa, using two or three scenario panels. The message should be escalation or added requirements, not fear.
must_include: one path that clearly becomes more formal or document-heavy

slots.anchor_heading: How it differs from the Schengen rule
slots.scene_brief: Show the UK visitor logic and the Schengen rolling-window logic as two different systems side by side. The image should make it obvious that these are not the same kind of rule or counter.
must_avoid: implying the UK uses the Schengen-style rolling counter

slots.anchor_heading: Is there a formal "180 days in 12 months" rule?
slots.scene_brief: Show the difference between a simple fixed-count myth and the more discretionary visitor-route reality. The composition should make the oversimplified '180 in 12 months' idea look too neat compared with the actual decision context.
must_include: one more nuanced or discretionary panel
must_avoid: treating the 180-day myth as the real system
dense legal text inside the image

slots.scene_brief: Show a U.S. stay authorization as a fixed or clearly bounded period tied to arrival, presence, and exit. The image should feel like stay-length planning rather than a generic visa document.
must_avoid: passport-visa page closeup as the only subject

slots.anchor_heading: ESTA and the Visa Waiver Program
slots.scene_brief: Show the contrast between the B1/B2 route and the ESTA/Visa Waiver route as two different travel-authorisation paths into the U.S. The image should communicate category choice, not legal detail overload.
must_include: two clearly distinct U.S. entry paths

slots.scene_brief: Show a trip strip where the entry day and exit day are both visibly included in the count. The entire point should be that the endpoints matter and are easy to misread.
must_avoid: long explanatory copy inside the image

slots.scene_brief: Show several travel-day counting systems side by side, such as visa, residency, or transit-related interpretations. The image should communicate that the same travel event can be counted differently depending on the rule set.
must_include: the same trip interpreted by multiple systems
must_avoid: one flat trip timeline with no comparison

slots.scene_brief: Show a concrete Schengen re-entry example where days come back one by one after a full 90-day stay. The image should feel like planning math made visible.
must_include: a fixed stay followed by days returning over time

slots.scene_brief: Show two or three planning mistakes people make when expecting Schengen days back, such as assuming a full reset or misreading how the rolling window frees days. The image should correct intuition through contrast.
must_avoid: single clean calendar with no scenario contrast
